intro_script: Welcome back! Today, June 10, 2026. We're diving into thirteen stories covering contact mechanics shaping dexterity, advanced robotic hands arriving on major platforms, and a new EU AI living lab kicking off innovation. Tune in!

items.title: Beyond Dexterity: Why Contact May Define the Next Era of Robotics
items.summary: Balloon twisting at ICRA 2026 demonstrated advanced robotic manipulation that goes beyond simple dexterity by mastering continuous physical interaction. This task requires both "motion intelligence" for sequential planning and "contact intelligence" to maintain stability amid deformable, slippery objects. AGILINK's system learned recovery from failures, mimicking human intuition regarding force regulation and slippage. The company also unveiled the OmniHand 3 Ultra-M hand, which features a direct-drive architecture and extensive tactile sensing to support this critical contact ability. Ultimately, successful complex manipulation relies heavily on rapid physical responsiveness alongside advanced sensory feedback.
items.audio_script: From IEEE Spectrum - Robotics, "Beyond Dexterity: Why Contact May Define the Next Era of Robotics." This report details advanced robotic manipulation capabilities that emphasize continuous physical interaction over mere dexterity. At ICRA 2026, researchers demonstrated balloon twisting, a task requiring both motion intelligence for sequential planning and contact intelligence to manage deformable and slippery materials. The system developed by AGILINK exhibited an ability to recover from mechanical failures, simulating human intuition regarding force control and slippage mitigation. Furthermore, the unveiling of the OmniHand 3 Ultra-M hand highlighted its direct-drive structure paired with comprehensive tactile sensing for enhanced contact abilities. Successful complex manipulation in robotics is thus shown to rely significantly on rapid physical responsiveness coupled with sophisticated sensory feedback mechanisms.

items.title: Sharpa brings dexterous robot hands to Nvidia and Unitree humanoid reference design
items.summary: Sharpa has integrated its Wave tactile robot hands into the Unitree H2 Plus humanoid robot reference design. This makes it the first dexterous humanoid platform utilizing Nvidia's Isaac GR00T framework with Sharpa's tactile technology. The collaboration aims to support robotics developers and researchers. Developers can now use this configuration for building advanced, multi-fingered manipulation systems. Ultimately, this combination advances accessible development for complex robotic tasks.
items.audio_script: From Robotics & Automation News, "Sharpa brings dexterous robot hands to Nvidia and Unitree humanoid reference design." This report details the integration of advanced tactile robotic hands onto a leading humanoid platform. The collaboration establishes a pioneering system that combines several high-level technological components for research purposes. Specifically, Sharpa has fitted its Wave tactile hands onto the Unitree H2 Plus humanoid robot reference design. This arrangement represents the first known dexterous humanoid platform incorporating both Nvidia’s Isaac GR00T framework and Sharpa's proprietary tactile sensing technology. The development provides developers with a ready-to-use configuration for building sophisticated, multi-fingered manipulation systems. This advance accelerates the accessibility of developing solutions for complex robotic tasks across various sectors.

items.title: Nebius and Nvidia launch Physical AI Living Lab for European robotics startups
items.summary: Nebius, an AI cloud company, has partnered with Nvidia to launch the Physical AI Living Lab. This six-month program is designed to support British and European robotics startups. Participants will gain access to Nvidia's physical AI development tools. Additionally, the lab provides Nebius's robust AI cloud infrastructure. These resources address the complex needs of early-stage robotics companies for large-scale simulation, synthetic data, and accelerated computing power.
items.audio_script: From Robotics & Automation News, "Nebius and Nvidia launch Physical AI Living Lab for European robotics startups." This announcement details a collaborative initiative providing significant development resources to emerging robotics companies across Britain and Europe. The program establishes a six-month living laboratory structure designed to bolster early-stage innovation in the sector. Participants will benefit from access to Nvidia's comprehensive physical AI development toolsets, complemented by Nebius’s established and robust AI cloud infrastructure. These combined assets are intended to directly address the intensive requirements of scaling robotics technology, specifically concerning large-scale simulation capabilities, synthetic data generation, and necessary accelerated computing power. This partnership signals a major commitment toward accelerating the practical deployment phase for advanced physical artificial intelligence systems across the continent.

items.title: Standard Bots raises $200M to expand U.S. manufacturing footprint
items.summary: Standard Bots has secured \$200 million in funding. This capital will be used to expand the company's manufacturing presence within the United States. Standard Bots believes that investments in robotics are crucial for enhancing the competitiveness of American industry globally. The announcement regarding this funding and expansion was first published on The Robot Report.
items.audio_script: From The Robot Report, "Standard Bots raises $200M to expand U.S. manufacturing footprint." The news reports that Standard Bots has successfully secured a substantial investment of two hundred million dollars. This capital infusion is earmarked specifically for broadening the company's operational and manufacturing presence within the United States borders. Company documentation suggests that such investments in advanced robotics technology are viewed by Standard Bots as fundamental to strengthening the global competitiveness of American industry sectors moving forward. The announcement detailing both the funding achievement and the subsequent expansion plans was initially released through publications associated with The Robot Report. This development signals continued investor confidence in domestic industrial automation, potentially signaling increased activity across related technological manufacturing supply chains.

items.title: Automation is Hiring Faster Than Schools Can Graduate Engineers
items.summary: Automation is currently functioning as a rapid hiring force across diverse sectors like manufacturing, energy grids, and research. Companies are deploying robots and intelligent systems at an accelerating rate. This technological advancement creates a significant demand for skilled engineers. However, the current pace of automation outstrips the rate at which educational institutions can graduate enough qualified professionals. This mismatch presents a substantial workforce challenge.
items.audio_script: From Robotics & Automation News, "Automation is Hiring Faster Than Schools Can Graduate Engineers." This report details a significant mismatch between the rapid deployment of automated systems and the supply of available skilled engineering talent. Across manufacturing, energy grids, and research sectors, companies are accelerating the integration of advanced robotics and intelligent operational systems. This technological surge mandates an increasing workforce capability that current educational output is not meeting. The core finding indicates that the pace of industry automation significantly outpaces the rate at which academic institutions can graduate qualified engineers, establishing a considerable workforce challenge. Industry stakeholders must now focus on upskilling initiatives to bridge this widening skills gap in the automated technology sector.

items.title: Wing and Walmart expand drone delivery network to seven new US metro areas
items.summary: Wing and Walmart are expanding their residential drone delivery network across the U.S. service footprint by adding seven new metropolitan areas. This expansion contributes to their overall goal of delivering goods via drone to 40 million Americans by 2027. New locations included in the rollout encompass cities such as Memphis, New Orleans, Philadelphia, and Phoenix. The joint venture aims to establish what it calls the largest residential drone delivery network in the country.
items.audio_script: From Robotics & Automation News, "Wing and Walmart expand drone delivery network to seven new US metro areas." The report details a significant expansion of the joint venture's residential drone delivery service across multiple American cities. This rollout incorporates an additional seven metropolitan locations into the operational footprint. Specific additions noted include Memphis, New Orleans, Philadelphia, and Phoenix among other markets participating in the coordinated launch. The initiative advances the stated objective of delivering goods via Unmanned Aerial Vehicles to forty million Americans by the year twenty twenty-seven, positioning it as a major national delivery network buildout. This growth signals continued commercial investment into last-mile automated logistics infrastructure across urban centers.

items.title: Festo launches GripperAI software to simplify flexible robot handling
items.summary: Festo launched GripperAI, an AI-powered software solution for advanced robotic handling. This technology allows robots to manage mixed, unfamiliar, or randomly placed products without intensive programming or complex vision systems. GripperAI automatically identifies the best gripping points for individual items. It streamlines the process of automating handling tasks in manufacturing settings. The goal is to provide manufacturers with greater flexibility in their automated systems.
items.audio_script: From Robotics & Automation News, "Festo launches GripperAI software to simplify flexible robot handling." This development centers on an artificial intelligence solution designed to enhance the capability of industrial robots in managing diverse and unstructured material handling tasks. The technology enables automated systems to operate effectively with mixed or randomly positioned products without requiring extensive programming or sophisticated dedicated vision infrastructure. Specifically, GripperAI automatically determines optimal gripping points for individual items within a bin or workspace. This process significantly streamlines complex automation implementations across various manufacturing environments. Consequently, the industry anticipates an increased level of operational flexibility in robotic handling solutions moving forward.

items.title: Mesoware raises $1.5 million funding to build AI-powered robots for manufacturing
items.summary: Mesoware secured $1.5 million in funding during a pre-seed round led by Pillar VC. The capital will support the company's development of advanced, AI-powered robotic solutions for manufacturing. Co-founder and CEO Joe Mattekatt stated that Mesoware aims to overcome scalability challenges in robotics. He highlighted falling hardware costs and advancing AI as key factors enabling this progress.
items.audio_script: From Robotics & Automation News, "Mesoware raises $1.5 million funding to build AI-powered robots for manufacturing." The report details the company's successful securing of new capital to accelerate its development of advanced robotic systems tailored for industrial settings. Mesoware obtained the $1.5 million through a pre-seed funding round, with Pillar VC leading the investment. Company officials indicated that these funds will specifically support building out sophisticated, AI-driven solutions designed to address current scalability limitations within robotics manufacturing. This progress is attributed by the firm to concurrent decreases in hardware costs and major advancements across artificial intelligence technology. The sector anticipates further integration of cost-effective, intelligent automation systems following this capital infusion.

items.title: Startup tests drug reversing age-related vision loss in humans
items.summary: Longevity startup Life Biosciences initiated human clinical trials for ER-100, a drug aiming to reverse age-related vision loss. The company reported that the drug successfully restored sight in monkeys. If these trials prove successful, it could potentially treat conditions like glaucoma and NAION by rejuvenating cells and reversing optic nerve damage. This research represents a potential advance in reversing other age-related declines as well.
items.audio_script: From thehustle.co, "Startup tests drug reversing age-related vision loss in humans." The report details a company initiating human clinical trials for a medication designed to reverse decline associated with aging eyesight. Life Biosciences announced that its drug candidate, ER-100, has already demonstrated success in restoring sight when tested on monkeys. These ongoing trials are structured to assess the drug's capacity to rejuvenate cells and potentially reverse optic nerve damage, which could address conditions such as glaucoma and NAION. Successful outcomes from this research mark a significant development, suggesting potential avenues for reversing other forms of age-related biological decline across various medical fields.

items.title: Apple iOS 27 Focuses on Speed and Cleanups Not Flashy Features
items.summary: iOS 27 is presented as a speed and cleanup release rather than one featuring flashy new features. Key updates include faster app launches (up to 30%) and significantly quicker photo loading times (up to 70%). Apple is extending iOS 27 support back to the iPhone 11 while introducing a user-controlled slider to adjust its Liquid Glass aesthetic. Functionally, iCloud Shared Albums now supports Android and Windows, and Maps receives an enhanced Flyover feature. These changes focus on performance improvements for a wide range of older devices.
items.audio_script: From tech.therundown.ai, "Apple iOS 27 Focuses on Speed and Cleanups Not Flashy Features." The update prioritizes performance enhancements over significant new visual additions. Key reported upgrades include accelerated app launching, with potential improvements noted up to thirty percent, alongside marked reductions in photo loading times of up to seventy percent. Furthermore, the operating system's support window is expanded back to the iPhone 11, and a user-controlled slider is introduced for adjusting the Liquid Glass aesthetic. Functionally, iCloud Shared Albums now extends compatibility across Android and Windows platforms, while Maps receives an improved Flyover feature rollout. These updates signal a renewed focus on optimizing device longevity and core functionality within the mobile ecosystem.

items.title: Güdel expands robotic grinding reach with integrated vertical and horizontal motion
items.summary: Automating grinding and surface finishing on fabricated parts is a classic 3D application suited for robotics. Current automation methods for large fabrications require multiple robots and complex repositioning of parts. This setup significantly increases cost and extends overall cycle times. Furthermore, these complex setups introduce new sources of variation into the process. A solution is needed to improve efficiency and reduce variability in robotic grinding operations.
items.audio_script: From Robotics & Automation News, "Güdel expands robotic grinding reach with integrated vertical and horizontal motion." This report details a technological advancement aimed at streamlining the automation of grinding and surface finishing processes on large fabricated components. Current industrial methods often necessitate the use of multiple robotic units alongside intricate part repositioning, leading to elevated operational costs and extended cycle times. The development introduces an integrated system that addresses these limitations by optimizing movement within complex geometries, thus improving overall efficiency and reducing process variation in robotic grinding applications. This represents a notable stride toward more comprehensive and streamlined automation solutions for advanced manufacturing sectors.

items.title: How JPL Keeps the 13-Year-Old Curiosity Rover Doing Science
items.summary: Despite being thirteen years old and operating in Mars' hostile environment, the Curiosity rover continues scientific missions through careful software updates from JPL engineers. Significant longevity was achieved by swapping out failing computer components and ingeniously using redundant flight software memory as functional storage for one processor. Current constraints include wear on the wheels due to sharp rocks and decreasing power from its aging RTG nuclear source. To manage limitations, operations focus on increasing energy efficiency by cycling systems off when not needed. The experience guides future missions toward early operator input during design to better understand dynamic power management requirements.
items.audio_script: From IEEE Spectrum - Robotics, "How JPL Keeps the 13-Year-Old Curiosity Rover Doing Science." The report details the remarkable efforts sustaining scientific operations on a decade-old robotic vehicle operating in Mars' challenging conditions. To maintain functionality, engineers at Jet Propulsion Laboratory executed critical software updates and component replacements. Significant longevity was achieved by utilizing redundant flight software memory as functional storage for one processor unit. Current operational constraints involve wheel wear from abrasive Martian terrain and the gradual power depletion associated with its radioisotope thermoelectric generator source. Management of these limitations necessitates a focus on increasing energy efficiency through systematic system cycling when they are not required for mission objectives. This sustained effort informs future design philosophies, emphasizing the integration of early operator input to better model dynamic power management requirements for subsequent space exploration endeavors.

items.title: Powering the future of robotics in Europe
items.summary: Europe is positioned to lead the next wave of robotics innovation through strategic investment and research initiatives. Developing robust frameworks for artificial intelligence and advanced hardware integration are key priorities across member states. Collaboration between academia, industry leaders, and governments is vital for commercializing novel robotic technologies. Furthermore, focusing on ethical guidelines and workforce reskilling will ensure responsible adoption throughout European industries. These combined efforts aim to establish Europe as a global powerhouse in the robotics sector.
items.audio_script: From Google DeepMind Blog, "Powering the future of robotics in Europe." The report outlines that Europe is strategically positioned to lead the next wave of robotics innovation through concerted investment and research initiatives across its member states. Key priorities involve developing robust frameworks for artificial intelligence alongside advanced hardware integration throughout the bloc. Successful commercialization of novel robotic technologies hinges on collaboration among academia, industry leaders, and governmental bodies. Additionally, establishing clear ethical guidelines and implementing comprehensive workforce reskilling programs are cited as crucial elements for ensuring responsible industrial adoption across Europe. These combined efforts aim to solidify the continent’s standing as a global powerhouse in the robotics sector. The developments signal a concentrated movement toward policy-driven hardware advancement within the international AI technology landscape.

items.title: Uncertainty-Aware Motion Planning for Autonomous Driving in Mixed Traffic Environment
items.summary: Motion planning for autonomous vehicles in mixed traffic demands anticipating uncertain human driver behaviors. Current methods treating predicted intents as certain states risk unsafe decisions due to inherent uncertainty. We propose Uncertainty-Aware Motion Planning (UAMP), which explicitly incorporates intent uncertainty into decision-making. UAMP first estimates proximity-aware uncertainty and builds an uncertainty-guided joint intent distribution. By using Uncertainty-Calibrated Value Learning, UAMP corrects value function biases related to uncertain predictions, significantly improving safety and comfort in simulations.

items.title: GHOST: Hierarchical Sub-Goal Policies for Generalizing Robot Manipulation
items.summary: GHOST is a framework designed for learning visuomotor manipulation policies that can generalize beyond their training data. It decomposes control into two parts: a high-level policy predicting the next sub-goal as a 3D end-effector pose distribution, and a low-level goal-conditioned controller executing specific actions. A spatial interface is introduced to condition image-based policies on these 3D goals by projecting them onto heatmaps in the image plane. This hierarchical structure significantly improves performance and robustness compared to flat Diffusion Policies across various tasks. Furthermore, this design allows for easy integration of human demonstrations without requiring noisy action retargeting.

items.title: Robotic Nonprehensile Object Transportation with a Hanging Tray
items.summary: Addresses the nonprehensile object transportation "waiter's problem" by using a real, rope-suspended hanging tray instead of rigidly tilting it. This setup allows the tray to behave like a three-dimensional pendulum while only actuating a 3 DOF mobile base, avoiding complex 6-DOF manipulator control. Experiments demonstrate that this dynamic swinging significantly reduces object sliding and liquid sloshing compared to static grasping methods. The system integrates an interactive demonstration utilizing computer vision to detect raised hands on people. Visual servoing is employed to direct the robot waiter toward the identified individuals for tray access.

items.title: Efficient-WAM: A 1B-Parameter World-Action Model with Low-Cost Future Imagination
items.summary: World-Action Models (WAMs) for embodied control are hindered by the high inference latency of photorealistic future visual prediction. Efficient-WAM addresses this by proposing a low-cost alternative that retains the benefits of future imagination. It achieves efficiency through a compact video expert, token-sparse video latents, and asymmetric video-action denoising. Instead of prioritizing visual fidelity, it uses future video prediction as a compact guidance signal for action generation. Testing on RoboTwin 2.0 shows that this 1B-parameter model maintains strong control while achieving a 30x speedup in latency down to around 100 ms.

items.title: Exploration of Foundation Model-Based Robots in Patient and Elderly Care
items.summary: Growing global demand for patient and elderly care is driving the integration of foundation models into robotics for personalized assistance. Current robotic systems primarily utilize these models for conversational and reasoning capabilities within socially assistive embodiments, though physical autonomy remains limited. Empirical studies show positive user engagement but report persistent reliability issues, including hallucinations. Evidence for clinical outcomes is currently limited, concentrating mostly on cognitive engagement rather than validated care improvements. Future research must focus on developing care-specific evaluation standards, ensuring accountable autonomy, and integrating technologies directly into existing care workflows.

items.title: What Demonstration Curation Metrics Do to Your Policy
items.summary: Research investigated whether metrics designed to detect defective training episodes improve subsequent behavior-cloning policies. Using a pick-and-place task with controlled structural defects, the study found that high defect-detection performance (AUROC of 0.804) resulted in a poor policy (13.3% success). Conversely, a metric with lower detection accuracy (AUROC of 0.638) yielded a near-oracle performing policy (90.0% success). The findings demonstrate that evaluation should focus on the resulting policy performance rather than solely the metrics used for defect flagging. Furthermore, the research cautioned that many current metrics improperly rely on episode length as a proxy for defects.

items.title: YUBI: Yielding Universal Bidigital Interface for Bimanual Dexterous Manipulation at Scale
items.summary: Developed Yielding Universal Bidigital Interface (YUBI), a finger-aligned gripper addressing ergonomic limitations of bulky handheld data collection systems like UMI. YUBI utilizes yielding, finger-driven actuation directly mapping human finger movements to jaw motion for intuitive control. Using this system, researchers created an extensive dataset comprising 8,434 hours across numerous tasks. Testing demonstrated YUBI's superiority over the UMI gripper in dexterity and versatility for complex bimanual manipulation tasks. The integrated stack, including hardware, software, and data, allows policy trained on YUBI to transfer across various robot platforms.

items.title: What Matters in Orchestrating Robot Policies: A Systematic Study of Hierarchical VLA Agents
items.summary: Hierarchical vision-language-action (Hi-VLA) systems use high-level language models to break down complex tasks into subgoals for low-level controllers, showing promise in robot manipulation. Current Hi-VLA designs lack unified principles regarding planner choice, controller connection, switching mechanisms, and observation representation. This paper systematically studies Hi-VLA design by unifying agents within an options-style control framework. The authors benchmark core design choices across varied tasks (short, long, reasoning) to distill actionable building principles. Applying these derived principles results in a significantly stronger system compared to flat or poorly designed hierarchical controls on both simulation and real hardware.

items.title: Locomotion analysis of a quadruped interacting with the lunar granular surface
items.summary: Designing quadruped robots for lunar exploration requires considering motor torques, energy use, and cost of transport across challenging terrains. Lunar surfaces consist of granular regolith, which significantly affects robot locomotion. Locomotion algorithms assuming rigid contacts fail in soft, granular environments, leading to instability. Researchers modeled the physical interaction between a robot foot and granular regolith within a simulation. The analysis compared policies trained on rigid versus soft contact models, finding that simulating regolith increases energy expenditure and causes qualitatively different gaits.
